title: Quiz: New Quantum Algorithmic Primitives
description: Test your understanding of block encodings, quantum signal processing, and QSVT

questions.question: What is a block encoding of a matrix A?

options.text: A decomposition of A into tensor products of smaller matrices
A larger unitary U where A appears as a sub-block after appropriate projections
An encoding of A's elements into quantum amplitudes
A compression of A using quantum error correction codes

questions.explanation: A block encoding embeds a (possibly non-unitary) matrix A into a larger unitary U, such that A can be recovered by projecting onto ancilla qubits in the |0> state.

questions.question: In an (alpha, m, epsilon)-block encoding, what does alpha represent?

options.text: The number of qubits in the system
The normalization factor (subnormalization) of the encoded matrix
The angle of rotation in the quantum circuit
The success probability of the encoding

questions.explanation: Alpha is the normalization factor such that A/alpha is what appears in the top-left block of the unitary. Larger alpha means more queries are needed for many applications.

questions.question: What does Quantum Signal Processing (QSP) allow you to implement?

options.text: Arbitrary quantum circuits using only single-qubit gates
Polynomial transformations of eigenvalues using a sequence of phase rotations
Classical signal processing algorithms on quantum computers
Communication between quantum and classical processors

questions.explanation: QSP implements polynomial transformations P(cos(theta)) by interleaving signal rotations with carefully chosen phase rotations.

questions.question: What constraint must a polynomial P satisfy to be implementable via QSP?

options.text: P must have integer coefficients
P must be definite parity (all even or all odd powers) and bounded by 1 on [-1,1]
P must be linear
P must have degree less than 10

questions.explanation: QSP polynomials must have definite parity (even or odd) and satisfy |P(x)| <= 1 for x in [-1, 1]. These constraints arise from unitarity requirements.

questions.question: What does QSVT transform in a block-encoded matrix?

options.text: The matrix elements directly
The eigenvalues of the matrix
The singular values of the matrix
The tensor rank of the matrix

questions.explanation: QSVT applies polynomial transformations to the singular values. For Hermitian matrices, singular values equal absolute eigenvalues, connecting to eigenvalue transformation.

questions.question: Which algorithms can be derived from the QSVT framework? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Hamiltonian simulation
HHL linear systems algorithm
Grover's search (amplitude amplification)
Shor's factoring algorithm

questions.explanation: QSVT unifies Hamiltonian simulation (implementing e^{-iHt}), HHL (implementing 1/x), and amplitude amplification (Chebyshev polynomials). Shor's algorithm uses different techniques based on QFT.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of the LCU (Linear Combination of Unitaries) block encoding method?

options.text: It requires no ancilla qubits
It naturally handles Hamiltonians expressed as sums of simple unitaries
It always produces normalization factor alpha = 1
It works only for sparse matrices

questions.explanation: LCU is ideal when H = sum_j alpha_j U_j for simple unitaries U_j (like Pauli strings). The normalization is sum_j |alpha_j|, and it uses log(L) ancillas for L terms.

questions.question: QSVT achieves optimal query complexity for problems like Hamiltonian simulation and matrix inversion.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. QSVT has been proven to achieve optimal query complexity (in terms of block encoding queries) for many quantum linear algebra tasks, matching known lower bounds.
